Bearing 315

The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | October-December 2002
www.CascadeOC.org

Current COC Officers and Coordinators

  • *Executive Board Member – Kean Williams, 425-931-3036, keanwill@yahoo.com

In Memorium: Aaron Charlop

Cascade Membership Coordinator Aaron Charlop lost his long battle with cancer, passing away on July 26 at the age of 38. Though he hadn't been able to participate in orienteering for some time, Aaron continued to play an important role in the club as Membership Coordinator, giving up his duties just a few months before his death. Aaron was a past recipient of Cascade's President's Award for his service to the club.

Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival 2003

Cascade and other regional clubs (CROC, CTOC, EWOC, SAMM) are planning a week-long orienteering festival in June of next year. Preliminary plans are to start with a two-day event in Cle Elum on June 21-22, continue with two days in Spokane, then on to Pullman for one day, finishing in McCall, Idaho on June 28-29. Stay tuned for more details in the coming months. If you are interested in volunteering, please contact Eric Bone (206-634-2279, eric.bone@attbi.com).

Another Order of COC O' Suits

Cascade will be placing another order for orienteering suits some time this fall or winter. If you are interested in getting a suit, please contact Jennifer Knowles (253-845-6588, jsknowles@msn.com) for pricing and sizing information.

[Image: A graphical mockup of the Cascade Orienteering Club O-suit design. It displays a long-sleeve top and matching pants, primarily dark blue, featuring a wide yellow band across the chest and shoulders outlined with bright green diagonal stripes. "Cascade Orienteering Club" is printed on the back of the shirt.]

25th Anniversary Celebration

We will mark our 25th anniversary this year with a celebration on November 24th at a place yet to be determined. Please plan to bring any old maps, photos, or club t-shirts to share. If you have any suggestions or would like to volunteer to help organize the event, please contact Eric Bone (206-634-2279, eric.bone@attbi.com).

This event will be held in conjunction with our annual elections. Please check our website in the coming weeks for more information.

Winter O' Series Begins in November

The 11th Winter O' Series begins on November 9 with an event at Woodland Park in Seattle. The series consists of seven events and finishes with the championship event at Lake Tradition in Issaquah on February 22, 2003. If you'd like to participate in the Series, just sign up for the longest course at any of the events. You will automatically be entered in the Winter O' Series. You will receive points based on your finish position at each event you attend. Your best 4 scores count towards your final total. The top 3 men and women receive awards, given out at the championship event. The Championships is a separate event, with separate awards. Please see the schedule page for other events in the series.

Meet Results

Bike-O, Ballard - July 21

Meet Director/Course Design: Harvey Friedman
Start/Finish: Anne York
Control Pickup: Harvey Friedman, Anne York

Having the registration/start/finish/socialization area at the SW corner of the Ballard Community Center block on NW 60th St. & 28th Ave. NW was so pleasant that 5 of the 18 people who participated last summer returned for more. Thanks to the Sprugel family and their friends, we set a new attendance record for bike-O with a grand total of 29 people who did at least 1 course. Pat Kelly did the ironman stint, bicyling the longest course and then running the 6 km. course. Ken Lew demonstrated that we only get better with age as he improved from 57:10 last year to 56:45 this year for same course.
— Harvey Friedman

Carkeek Park, Seattle - August 24

Meet directors: Terry Farrah/Bill Cusworth
Courses: Eric Bone
Event setup: Ali Woolwich
Registration: Mark Ellison-Taylor
Start: Anna Urbanova, Jan Urban
Finish: Eugen Groff, Kean Williams
Control Pickup: Eugen Groff, Eric Bone, Terry Farrah, Kean Williams

A fine sunny August day for this meet. Although Eric, arriving a tad late with the maps, was hanging controls for course 3 with runners at his heels, and some course 3 runners complained about brambly bits, the meet was a general success. It worked well for us to hold registration at the southeast corner of the park—parking was fairly easy, and the spot was uncrowded — though we did shell out $100 for a portable toilet since there were no permanent ones nearby.
— Terry Farrah

Asia Pacific Orienteering Carnival - July 4-14, Alberta Canada

Held this year in Alberta, Canada, the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Carnival (or APOC) is held in a different Pacific Rim country every two years. This year's carnival encompassed ten days of orienteering events, including the Canadian Classic Championships, the Canadian Short Course Championships, and the North American Championships. There were also fun events, like the Park-O series, which were held at parks and university campuses and featured short courses and fast running.

The relay, held at Mt. Laurie, in the foothills of the Rockies west of Calgary, was one of the highlights of the week, with more than 140 teams from all over the world taking part. There was some controversy, however, as the advanced leg in the 4-point category was printed on both sides of the map. There was no announcement about this unusual condition and, in the heat of the competition, some runners did not think to turn the map over for the other half of the course. Several teams were disqualified for not completing the course, while others lost time returning to the start to exchange their “defective” maps.

All in all, though, APOC2002 was a great success, with wonderful maps, interesting terrain, and excellent organization. The next APOC will take place in Kazakhstan.

block_index.text: The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | October-December 2002 www.CascadeOC.org
Vice President/Training Coordinator – Eric Bone, 206-634-2279, eric.bone@attbi.com
Land Use Coordinator - Dave Tallent, 425-488-3691, dtallent@gtemail.net

Cascade Membership Coordinator Aaron Charlop lost his long battle with cancer, passing away on July 26 at the age of 38. Though he hadn't been able to participate in orienteering for some time, Aaron continued to play an important role in the club as Membership Coordinator, giving up his duties just a few months before his death. Aaron was a past recipient of Cascade's President's Award for his service to the club.

Cascade and other regional clubs (CROC, CTOC, EWOC, SAMM) are planning a week-long orienteering festival in June of next year. Preliminary plans are to start with a two-day event in Cle Elum on June 21-22, continue with two days in Spokane, then on to Pullman for one day, finishing in McCall, Idaho on June 28-29. Stay tuned for more details in the coming months. If you are interested in volunteering, please contact Eric Bone (206-634-2279, eric.bone@attbi.com).
Another Order of COC O' Suits

Cascade will be placing another order for orienteering suits some time this fall or winter. If you are interested in getting a suit, please contact Jennifer Knowles (253-845-6588, jsknowles@msn.com) for pricing and sizing information.

[Image: A graphical mockup of the Cascade Orienteering Club O-suit design. It displays a long-sleeve top and matching pants, primarily dark blue, featuring a wide yellow band across the chest and shoulders outlined with bright green diagonal stripes. "Cascade Orienteering Club" is printed on the back of the shirt.]

We will mark our 25th anniversary this year with a celebration on November 24th at a place yet to be determined. Please plan to bring any old maps, photos, or club t-shirts to share. If you have any suggestions or would like to volunteer to help organize the event, please contact Eric Bone (206-634-2279, eric.bone@attbi.com).
This event will be held in conjunction with our annual elections. Please check our website in the coming weeks for more information.
Winter O' Series Begins in November

The 11th Winter O' Series begins on November 9 with an event at Woodland Park in Seattle. The series consists of seven events and finishes with the championship event at Lake Tradition in Issaquah on February 22, 2003. If you'd like to participate in the Series, just sign up for the longest course at any of the events. You will automatically be entered in the Winter O' Series. You will receive points based on your finish position at each event you attend. Your best 4 scores count towards your final total. The top 3 men and women receive awards, given out at the championship event. The Championships is a separate event, with separate awards. Please see the schedule page for other events in the series.
Meet Director/Course Design: Harvey Friedman Start/Finish: Anne York Control Pickup: Harvey Friedman, Anne York

Having the registration/start/finish/socialization area at the SW corner of the Ballard Community Center block on NW 60th St. & 28th Ave. NW was so pleasant that 5 of the 18 people who participated last summer returned for more. Thanks to the Sprugel family and their friends, we set a new attendance record for bike-O with a grand total of 29 people who did at least 1 course. Pat Kelly did the ironman stint, bicyling the longest course and then running the 6 km. course. Ken Lew demonstrated that we only get better with age as he improved from 57:10 last year to 56:45 this year for same course. — Harvey Friedman

Meet directors: Terry Farrah/Bill Cusworth Courses: Eric Bone Event setup: Ali Woolwich Registration: Mark Ellison-Taylor Start: Anna Urbanova, Jan Urban Finish: Eugen Groff, Kean Williams Control Pickup: Eugen Groff, Eric Bone, Terry Farrah, Kean Williams

A fine sunny August day for this meet. Although Eric, arriving a tad late with the maps, was hanging controls for course 3 with runners at his heels, and some course 3 runners complained about brambly bits, the meet was a general success. It worked well for us to hold registration at the southeast corner of the park—parking was fairly easy, and the spot was uncrowded — though we did shell out $100 for a portable toilet since there were no permanent ones nearby. — Terry Farrah
Bo Powell Memorial / Ft. Steilacoom Park - Sept. 7
(Note: * indicates second course. + indicates Bo Powell Memorial Trophy winner)
Asia Pacific Orienteering Carnival - July 4-14, Alberta Canada

Held this year in Alberta, Canada, the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Carnival (or APOC) is held in a different Pacific Rim country every two years. This year's carnival encompassed ten days of orienteering events, including the Canadian Classic Championships, the Canadian Short Course Championships, and the North American Championships. There were also fun events, like the Park-O series, which were held at parks and university campuses and featured short courses and fast running.

The relay, held at Mt. Laurie, in the foothills of the Rockies west of Calgary, was one of the highlights of the week, with more than 140 teams from all over the world taking part. There was some controversy, however, as the advanced leg in the 4-point category was printed on both sides of the map. There was no announcement about this unusual condition and, in the heat of the competition, some runners did not think to turn the map over for the other half of the course. Several teams were disqualified for not completing the course, while others lost time returning to the start to exchange their “defective” maps.
All in all, though, APOC2002 was a great success, with wonderful maps, interesting terrain, and excellent organization. The next APOC will take place in Kazakhstan.
July 4 - University of Calgary, Calgary • July 5 - Canada Olympic Park, Calgary • July 11 - University of Alberta, Edmonton A series of short courses. Best 2 of 3 events counts.
(Day 1: Fallen Timber Meadows • Day 2: Mt. Laurie)
APOC Relay Championships - July 8, Mt. Laurie
Canadian Short Course Championships - July 12, Oskahtak
(best 5 of 6 events) (For complete APOC2002 results, visit www.apoc2002.com)
